parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: My shoulders have felt like they are carrying a stack of wet bricks for a while now, and it just won't shake off today. I keep rubbing at my temples hoping the pressure will ease up, but it just sits there. Every little task feels heavier than it should, and I catch myself staring at the wall for way too long. I know I should push through, but my body is just dragging its feet. It is weird how a simple Tuesday can feel this heavy.
turns.model_response:
That sounds really heavy and exhausting. When your body feels tight and weighed down like that, even ordinary things can feel unusually hard.

It may help to treat today like a low-energy day on purpose:
- shrink the next task to something tiny
- drink some water and eat something simple if you can
- give your shoulders/jaw a few slow unclenches
- step away from the wall-staring for just one small action, like standing up or opening a window
- let yourself do the bare minimum for a bit, without judging it

You don’t have to force “pushing through” if your system is clearly asking for a softer pace. If you want, I can help you sort out whether this feels more like stress, burnout, a headache/tension thing, or something else.

turns.user_message: This tightness in my chest has been hanging around for weeks now, and it refuses to leave no matter how much I rest. I wake up with my jaw already clenched, and my lower back aches before my feet even hit the floor. I try to stretch it out, but the tension just snaps right back into place. It feels like my whole frame is locked in place, waiting for a break that never actually comes. I am just tired of carrying this invisible weight around everywhere I go.
turns.model_response:
That sounds really exhausting — like your body has been stuck in “brace for impact” mode for far too long. I’m sorry you’ve been carrying that around. When tension shows up as chest tightness, clenched jaw, and back pain for weeks, it can feel relentless and scary.

A couple important things:
- **Chest tightness that lasts for weeks is worth getting checked by a medical professional**, especially if it’s new, worsening, or coming with shortness of breath, dizziness, sweating, pain spreading to your arm/jaw/back, or nausea. If any of those are happening, it’s safest to seek urgent care right away.
- If stress or anxiety is part of this, that’s still real physical strain — not “just in your head.”

If you want something small to try right now, you could:
- Unclench your jaw and let your tongue rest gently on the roof of your mouth
- Drop your shoulders on an exhale
- Try a slow exhale longer than your inhale for a minute or two
- Put a warm pack on your jaw, neck, or lower back if that feels good

But more than anything, I think you deserve support for this — both physical and emotional. If you want, I can help you sort through whether this feels more like stress, panic, burnout, or something else, or help you make a plan for what to say to a doctor.

turns.user_message: My hands actually tremble when I try to focus, and my stomach drops like a stone every single time I sit down to start. The exhaustion has sunk so deep into my bones that even blinking feels like a massive chore. I feel completely hollowed out, like there is nothing left inside to fuel my movements. Every muscle screams at me to just stop, but I keep forcing myself forward anyway. I am running on absolute fumes and my body is finally staging a full rebellion.
turns.model_response:
That sounds brutally exhausting. What you’re describing—trembling, stomach drops, feeling hollowed out and forced to keep going—really sounds like your system is past its limit, not like you’re “just being weak.”

Right now, the most important thing is to reduce the load as much as you can:
- Stop pushing if at all possible, even briefly.
- Sit or lie down somewhere safe.
- Drink some water, and if you haven’t eaten in a while, have something small and easy if you can.
- Try a few slow breaths: in for 4, out for 6, just a handful of times.
- If you can, tell one person: “I’m not okay and I need help.”

If this has been building for a while, or if you’re having chest pain, trouble breathing, fainting, severe dizziness, or feel like you might collapse, please seek urgent medical help right away.

If you want, I can stay with you and help you figure out the very next tiny step—no big plan, just the next one.
